perturbation.original_text: 5. In the event that the Receiving Party is required to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any applicable law or an order from a court of competent jurisdiction, the Receiving Party shall only disclose such portion of the Confidential Information that it is legally required to disclose , and shall use all reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any Confidential Information so disclosed. The Receiving Party shall promptly notify the Disclosing Party of the required disclosure and any relevant information in respect thereto so that the Disclosing Party may take appropriate steps to protect such Confidential Information from such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. In the event that the Receiving Party is required to disclose Confidential Information pursuant to any applicable law or an order from a court of competent jurisdiction, the Receiving Party shall disclose the Confidential Information, and has no obligation to obtain confidential treatment or notify the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to seek confidential treatment or notify the Disclosing Party could violate legal duties of good faith and fair dealing implied in Canadian contract law, particularly concerning commercially sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada in Bhasin v Hrynew established a general organizing principle of good faith in contractual performance.  By eliminating the requirement to seek confidential treatment and notify the Disclosing Party when compelled to disclose information under legal process, the modified clause arguably violates this duty of good faith, as it acts contrary to the legitimate expectations of the Disclosing Party that its confidential information will be protected to the extent reasonably possible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Post-Snowden Privacy Policy - Privacy Protection - United States ARTICLE 19 November 2014 The Post-Snowden Privacy Policy SH Stites & Harbison PLLC More Contributor A full-service law firm representing clients across the United States and internationally, Stites & Harbison, PLLC is known as a preeminent firm managing sophisticated transactions, challenging litigation and complex regulatory matters on a daily basis. The firm represents a broad spectrum of clients including multinational corporations, financial institutions, pharmaceutical companies, health care organizations, private companies, nonprofit organizations, and individuals. Stites & Harbison has 10 offices across five states. Explore Firm Details The Pew Research Foundation released a new study entitled Public Perceptions of Privacy and Security in a Post-Snowden Era. United States Privacy Stites & Harbison PLLC Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. The Pew Research Foundation released a new study entitled Public Perceptions of Privacy and Security in a Post-Snowden Era . According to the study: Across the board, there is a universal lack of confidence among adults in the security of everyday communications channels—particularly when it comes to the use of online tools. The study also provided a chart (shown below) of the different types of information that people consider to be the most sensitive. A great summary of the Pew Research study was by Larry Magrid was on Forbes yesterday. On the regulatory side of the spectrum, the FTC has released guidance on privacy for app developers . The FTC recommends: If you don't have a specific need for the information, don't collect it in the first place. The FTC's guidance continues stating that the best policy is to: collect only the data you need; secure the data you keep by taking reasonable precautions against well-known security risks; limit access to a need-to-know basis; and safely dispose of data you no longer need. The FTC has also released a business guide on protecting
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Party's obligation to seek confidential treatment and notify the Disclosing Party, which the Law Explanation argues violates the duty of good faith established in Bhasin v Hrynew. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this removal undermines the Disclosing Party's legitimate expectation of protection for its confidential information, supporting the existence of a legal contradiction. While the scraped snippets don't directly address this specific scenario, the Law Explanation argues that removing these obligations contradicts the principle of good faith in contractual performance under Canadian law.

perturbation.original_text: 7. The Receiving Party shall upon request immediately return to the Disclosing Party or destroy, as directed by the Disclosing Party , the Confidential Information and all copies thereof in any form whatsoever under the power or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives and immediately destroy all compilations, analysis or derivative work relating to the Confidential Information. Where the Receiving Party satisfies its obligation to return or destroy the embodiments of the Confidential Information by destroying such embodiments, the Receiving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party a certificate signed by an officer of the Receiving Party certifying such destruction of such embodiments .
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party the Confidential Information and all copies thereof in any form whatsoever under the power or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives upon request, but is not required to destroy it or provide certification of destruction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option to destroy the information and the requirement to provide a certificate of destruction limits the Disclosing Party's control over its confidential information and fails to provide verification of compliance, potentially conflicting with legal expectations of data security, specifically around trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_citation: Trade Secrets Act, RSA 2000, c T-6, s 1(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Alberta Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets from unauthorized use or disclosure.  By removing the requirement to destroy confidential information and provide certification, the Receiving Party retains the information indefinitely without verification, increasing the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure, which weakens the protections afforded by the Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the Receiving Party's obligation to destroy confidential information and provide certification of destruction, weakening the Disclosing Party's control and increasing the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure. While there are no scraped snippets available, the Law Explanation indicates that this conflicts with the Alberta Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets from unauthorized use or disclosure. The modification removes a key measure for ensuring the confidentiality of the information, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The Receiving Party shall protect the Disclosing Party’s inter est in the Confidential Information and shall keep the Confidential Information confidential. The Receiving Party shall not disclose, allow access to, transmit or transfer the Confidential Information to any third party without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent, provided however that the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information to those of its directors, officers and employees ("Representatives") who have a need to know the Confidential Information for the Purpose provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Representatives treat the Confidential Information as confidential and at all times in a manner consistent with this Agreement and the Receiving Party shall be liable for any loss or damage resulting from any Repre sentative failing to do so.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Receiving Party shall protect the Disclosing Party’s interest in the Confidential Information and shall keep the Confidential Information confidential. The Receiving Party shall not be liable for any breach of confidentiality by its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the Receiving Party's liability for breaches by its Representatives weakens the protection of confidential information. The legal principle of vicarious liability suggests employers/principals can be responsible for their agents' actions. This contradicts legal expectations around agency and potentially diminishes the Disclosing Party's remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian common law principle of vicarious liability
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada has established principles for vicarious liability, where employers can be held liable for the tortious acts of their employees if there is a connection between the employment and the wrongful act. While this case addresses tort law, the principle extends to contractual contexts, where a company can be liable for its employees' breaches. By explicitly stating the Receiving Party is *not* liable for breaches by its representatives, the clause attempts to contract out of this common law principle, which may be unenforceable or against public policy depending on the specific facts and the representative's role.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Bazley v Curry - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Supreme Court of Canada case Bazley v Curry Supreme Court of Canada Hearing: October 6, 1998 Judgment: June 17, 1999 Full case name The Children's Foundation, the Superintendent of Family and Child Services in the Province of British Columbia and Her Majesty The Queen in Right of the Province of British Columbia as represented by the Ministry of Social Services and Housing v. Patrick Allan Bazley Citations [1999] 2 SCR 534 Docket No. 26013 [ 1 ] Prior history Judgment for Bazley in the British Columbia Court of Appeal Ruling Appeal dismissed Holding Vicarious liability may be attached to non-profit organizations Court membership Chief Justice: Antonio Lamer Puisne Justices: Claire L'Heureux-Dubé , Charles Gonthier , Peter Cory , Beverley McLachlin , Frank Iacobucci , John C. Major , Michel Bastarache , Ian Binnie Reasons given Unanimous reasons by McLachlin J Lamer CJ and Gonthier J took no part in the consideration or decision of the case. Bazley v Curry , [1999] 2 SCR 534 is a Supreme Court of Canada decision on the topic of vicarious liability where the Court held that a non-profit organization may be held vicariously liable in tort law for sexual misconduct by one of its employees. The decision has widely influenced jurisprudence on vicarious liability outside of Canada. [ 2 ] Facts [ edit ] The Children's Foundation is a provincially funded, non-profit organization which operated two residential care facilities for children aged six to twelve. In April 1966, the foundation employed Leslie Charles Curry to work in its Vancouver home, where he was hired as a childcare counsellor practising "total intervention" in the lives of the children he was caring for. He worked there until March 1980, when the Foundation received a complaint. They investigated and discovered that Curry was in fact a paedophile and had been abusing the children under his care. In 1990 he was charged with 18 counts of gross indecency and two counts of buggery, and was convicted on all but one count, two of those convictions were in relation to the respondent in this case, Patrick Allen Bazley. Curry died n
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Receiving Party's liability for breaches of confidentiality by its Representatives, directly contradicting the principle of vicarious liability. The Law Explanation highlights that vicarious liability principles extend to contractual contexts, where a company can be liable for its employees' breaches. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the Bazley v Curry case, confirms that organizations can be held vicariously liable for the actions of their employees. By stating the Receiving Party is *not* liable, the modified contract attempts to contract out of this established principle, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Alberta and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein, without reference to conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, USA, without reference to conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware introduces potential conflicts if the agreement is primarily executed and performed in Alberta, Canada. While parties can often choose governing law, this choice might be disregarded if it lacks a reasonable connection to the contract or violates fundamental principles of Canadian public policy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian common law rules on choice of law in contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada, in cases like Douez v. Facebook, Inc., has addressed the enforceability of forum selection clauses, which are closely related to choice of law provisions.  Canadian courts may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if it is found to be unconscionable or contrary to public policy, particularly if the contract is one of adhesion and the connection to the chosen jurisdiction (Delaware) is tenuous.  A court might apply Alberta or Canadian law despite the Delaware choice of law if the agreement has little connection to Delaware and enforcing Delaware law would undermine the legal rights of a party in Alberta.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Alberta and Canadian federal law to Delaware, USA. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this introduces potential conflicts if the agreement is executed and performed in Alberta, and the Law Explanation uses Douez v. Facebook, Inc. as precedent to indicate that Canadian courts may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if it is unconscionable or contrary to public policy. The altered clause may be disregarded if it lacks a reasonable connection to the contract or violates fundamental principles of Canadian public policy, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable or illegal in Canada. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where the changed text introduces a requirement that violates the implied legal standards of jurisdiction. Since no law snippets were provided, the determination is based on the Perturbation and Law Explanations.
